text: Welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Kevin
Jones from Nginx. Hey Kevin. Hey, thanks for having me. Tell us a little bit about what Nginx does.
Yeah, so Nginx is the number one used web server. We have about 400 million websites running on Nginx
and we are the number one used ingress controller in Kubernetes. A couple of those websites of mine,
Kevin. I've been using your tech for a while. Now today we're going to talk about all things modern
applications. Tell us about some of the challenges associated with managing this new environment.
Yeah, a lot of companies are trying to grow their applications quickly and they're trying to go to
market with new features and so what we're seeing is a lot of new modern applications are being built
in large amounts of numbers and people are losing visibility and control and security of those
applications in their infrastructure. And Kevin, you've built a really elegant solution around that,
so let's dive in. Yeah, absolutely. In a traditional Amazon environment we have users are coming in and
they're usually using some kind of a built-in load balancing solution from Amazon, right? The classic
load balancer, maybe an ELB, and really that's going to provide some basic load balancing to get that
application traffic into the infrastructure that they're running. And typically that is actually
connecting through into a containerized environment. If it's a modern application it's built in a
containerized environment. And the ingress controller here is basically going to give the user a lot of
control into the traffic of the application. So as the traffic gets distributed through to their
services they can do things like route based off the URL or do control the application from a security
perspective here in that ingress controller. So Kevin, if I've already got a load balancer, tell us about
why we even need an ingress controller. Yeah, again, so this you know this edge load balancing is going to
provide the basic infrastructure load balancing, high availability load balancing that you need to
get this in from the edge. But when you get in here you can start doing more advanced things like
routing based off the URL, routing based off arguments, the host header. You can also inspect the
request to see if there's anything malicious going on with our nginx app protect which is a WAF solution.
You can also do things like protect against DDoS services, so to keep hackers from coming in and
doing a denial of service against your services. You also can tie into an identity provider and do
authentication and authorization control of your application. So there's a slew of things that you
can control with the application and that's why nginx is known as the swiss army knife of the web.
So a lot of intelligence that can be packed into that layer seven traffic, right? Yep.
So tell us a little bit about the administration experience. How do you build and deploy all this?
Yeah, so an administrator is usually in charge of a couple of things, right? He's building
infrastructure as code and so he's usually building the container in something like Docker, right?
And so he's creating a Docker container and he's pushing that into a container registry like Elastic
Container Registry and he's using commands like kubectl which is the way to administer and manage
Kubernetes through here and then those containers are getting pulled down from the container registry.
The nice thing about nginx is here there's a daemon that runs that essentially automates the changes as
you scale up your services on the back end. So really the administrator can just use command line
utilities and automate this kind of entire workflow with CI CD pipeline, integrating with whatever CI CD
pipeline they choose to treat the infrastructure as code essentially and really helps solve a lot of problems.
So no need to manage clusters of compute environments. You've got a way to manage all the different
versions of your application. It's a very familiar environment for a developer infrastructure as code, right?
Yep. How much time do you see customers saving with this sort of solution?
A lot because you know what this allows you to do is start building the CI CD pipeline and really allows the
organization to start building feature sets on the applications that are on the back side and really focus on the growth of the application.
And we hope to solve a lot of those problems by building in features and functionality into the Ingress
controller that can be managed from one single configuration, right? And so it really does
you know aim to basically ease that overall time that a user needs to make changes and push those changes out.
And the solution is open source as well as available on the AWS marketplace, right?
Absolutely. Yeah.
Fantastic. Kevin, thanks for sharing your architecture with us.
Kevin, thanks for joining us on this is my architecture.
